commentBody: First? Haha I can’t even solve the puzzle yet.
Too easy. I solved in minus 24 hours 20 minutes.
Fitting seeing brain ROT and AI SLOP in the same crossword

11D.

That is all.

Fun puzzle today, though Monday easy.  It might be my PR if my Wednesday PR weren't set to a fake time.  (Crossword IT how do I fix that?!)

Love seeing the AI SLOP answer.  The theme answers were also cool.  My last square was not remembering it was Chris ISAAK as opposed to ISAAC.

A lot of good cluing today.  Just really, really easy.
I was thinking X a strike in bowling! Ten points.
Congratulations on your first puzzle! It was a fun one!
No, I don't get it. It is frustrating when the clues make no sense ever after I figure out all the answers. This is such  a case.
congratulations! Your first of many I'm sure.
Attaboy! Clever little Wednesday theme. Even though was easy for a Wednesday, the theme made it worthwhile. I liked the "mother of a stable family" clue.😊

The theme is so clever it's a shame the cluing and fill for the rest was so very pedestrian and Mondayish (exception for AI SLOP), but it's publish or perish, right? Day or night, love it or leave it, friend or foe, sooner or later, win or lose, I'll be delightedly solving each NYT as it comes, with neither fear nor favor. Well, OK, I'll favor some over others.  But no fear. Except for Sam Ezersky. I'd gladly fear a Saturday Ezersky. 

Also liked the RETRO sensibility of Mickey Rourke, Jessica Alba and Chris Isaak, although I think the Deerhunter clue is (don't mess with me Steve L.!) misguided: the movie is overwhelmingly set in the US, principally in SW Pennsylvania and nearby parts of WV and OH; of the three hours or so, only about 30-40 minutes are set in "Nam." But that's enough, I guess. The clue/answer is hit or miss; I could take it or leave it, but for me it's not a do or die moment.

This was a very nice puzzle, Mason. That you are making your debut at 16 with only a year of crossword experience isn't too shabby either. I do wish the editors had either slotted it on a Tuesday or toughened up the non-theme clues (or both).
Great debut, Mason!  Glad you didn’t give up.  If you can construct a puzzle like this as a sophomore, the SAT will be EASYPEASY!  More puzzles, please.
Congratulations, such an achievement.

Hyunjin,

Fellow (left/best) coaster here. 
Great concept and nice execution of clues for the themers.  

I’m sure you read these comments enough to know that criticism of ‘easy clues’ doesn’t mean the grid was bad. Comments are solve experiences, which include concept, fill, and clue difficulty. 

I’m not sure why I’m writing this so pedantically. You are the published constructor. Not me. But you are kid. Like 1/4 my age. 

So I want to say. That was a D1 grid. You get an A for me.  Low key. Aura.  Glazing…

Uncle weak sauce.

This constructor's debut puzzle wasn't especially difficult for this old-time solver. But as someone who has a hard time remembering anything from age sixteen (over 50 years ago) other than how pretty the girls were ...

Bravo! 

I know I couldn't have done it back then, and I'm sure you can only get better at making things more difficult for us.

(Let's hope the editors step up to that challenge?)
I got the Magic setting clue by thinking of Magic Johnson, not the Orlando team. So I think it was a great clue, in keeping with the theme.
An impressive debut from a new constructor. Amazed that he is still in high school! Looking forward to more from Mr. Lee in years to come.
Congratulations, Mason! What a fun solve. This experience would make a killer college essay.

Congrats on your debut, Mason!
7 puzzles before you are  published and 16 years of age? Mason, you are way ahead of the curve on both, well deserved kudos. This was actually a really brilliant tight theme. I’m sorry I don’t think the editors did you any favors with the level of cluing they allowed today, it really didn’t give me time to savor your theme or this nice grid, but regardless I enjoyed sussing out the theme.

I did have RASH before ITCH which held me up for just a minute otherwise this might have been my fastest Wednesday.

Note to self—Mickey ROoney did not get an Oscar nom for “The Wrestler”.

Best clue today: “First in a race”—ADAM

Terrific debut, again, kiddo and I expect many more.

Latest exhibit in the “puzzles are definitely getting easier” debate
Friday (!) themed puzzle (!!) Feb 19th 2016.
I rest my case.
Good one today, congrats on the debut.

I enjoyed how this theme turns the Schrödinger idea backwards.

You know, where the clue would be 
Right or Left
and the answer would be
[D/L]ibs

(yes, it took me this long to come up with another example)

Mason’s way means no one has to struggle with how to enter [D/L] in the grid, and no angry comments! Genius!
Not bad. It helped me that I figured out the theme quickly. Enjoyed the several ways to lead a race. Maybe I will challenge my students to try and get a crossword entered in the NY times

A Rebus puzzle, with the Rebuses in the clues.

Already filled in.

Kind of a starter rebus puzzle.

AT IT carries no sense of "obediently", so I am puzzled why it would be clued as "Working obediently".

It also carries no sense of "working", just of *doing* something.

I bet someone confused this phrase with "on it".

Best I've got is [F/P]ACT

TRUTHORDARE

Those are quite hard to come up with. Kudos to the setter.

Was thinking today is pozo was kind of strange. Like a Monday trying to be a Thursday. Then I sawt the constructor notes. It's pretty ambitious to be even doing puzzles at that age. 
Anyway enjoyable but easy.
Fantastic construction, mighty impressive for a high-schooler

I loved this puzzle. Every one of the theme answers made me smile. So clever!

The only place I was almost stuck was ISAAK/SKOR. I don’t know the person, and only know the candy from past crosswords 😅

Congratulations and ATTABOY Mason Hyunjin Lee. Your debut puzzle completed my 5 year solving streak! ⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️
That was a clever and tight theme. I had rasH before ITCH and I misspelled ROaRKE before ROURKE (I always get that wrong for some reason). 
I imagine we will be seeing more of your work! 
Signed, a fellow Angeleno

userLocation: Hong Kong sometimes; Portland this month
commentBody:
@SamCorbin

One of my favourite irreversible binomials is in the British English expression “as different as chalk and cheese.”  This is pretty much unknown in the U.S. so it was unfamiliar to me when I first heard it in Australia.   I thought it was rather cool so I used it myself, but got it backward and said “different as cheese and chalk” which brought howls and ridicule from my Aussie colleagues.  Irreversible ind
Yay Mason! Great puzzle and fun theme! Looking forward to more of your work.

(T/N)oday
NOW OR NEVER

Classy theme, only got it w Sam's help

Theme served as a cute bonus to a breezy solve, which makes history as my Wed PB

[L/M]ark

HELLORHIGHWATER  ?

Here’s something you know but you probably don’t know you know. I was reminded of this by the mention of irreversible binomials in the Wordplay column. The thing is, adjectives come in a particular order. Don’t believe me? Just ask a little old lady or a sober Californian linguist. If you still don’t believe me, how would it feel to ask an old little lady or a Californian sober linguist? Just feels wrong, right? We don’t realise it, but we naturally put adjectives in the “right” order. With thanks to Wikipedia for the handy list, that order is: opinion, size, age or shape, colour, origin, material, purpose. So Eeyore might be clutching a sad little wrinkled blue plastic birthday balloon. It would be all wrong if he had a plastic blue wrinkled birthday little sad balloon, wouldn’t it? My god, I salute English learners! You are amazing. (For more on this see the section on “Order” on the Wikipedia adjectives page: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adjective)

(Turn)pikes do not get their name because they are long and thin, although geographically and cartographically they are, but because of the turnpikes  (a kind of gate) that historically controlled access to them. 

That is all. Please continue.

A very small nit. I think “Working obediently” solves to “on it”, not AT IT. 

If my boss says “Petrol, i need that report on my desk by morning”, i can reply “On it!”  By which i mean i am working obediently. I couldn’t say “at it” (although i could say “hard at it!”)

“At it” is commonly clued as “fighting” or something similar. I concede that “at it” can also mean “engaged in an activity” or “focused on something”, but it doesn’t have the connotation of “obediently”, and it’s not necessarily “working”, unless you expand it to “hard at it”, as above. 

Am i just a pedant?

Congratulations - quite a feat.
Unfortunately I read the middle bracket as a J - so the hint at the theme was very mysterious, but I was able to solve regardless.

This was one of the most straightforward Wednesdays for me. I had to look up KMART and SKOR and the crossers did the rest - it took me a while to remember what 'keister' means. It's not a word I've used. I've learned it might be related to 'kist', used in Shetland for a box or chest.
Fun ride!     Theme clues and answers gave me a good chuckle.  Nice debut!!

I'm glad they didn't clue 30D as that elevator guy. Instead, Singer OTIS Redding who had many many hits. He wrote "(Sitting on) The Dock of the Bay" and "Respect." Wikipedia says his signature song is "Try a Little Tenderness." "Young girls, they do get wearied Wearing the same shaggy dress..." https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UnPMoAb4y8U BTW, for me three days in a row with no lookups. Yea!!!
I got hung up on 34D. CHEAPMEAL (aka McDonalds, altho lately not) fit, which made left 50A as PAEBO? A new word for paleo diet? Anyways, I liked the split clues / answers
To construct any crossword is an accomplishment to do it at 16 is remarkable, even if it was easy. Congrats!
Simple and fun Wednesday.
Loved it! So clever. I first solved 27A and I took a moment to savour the brilliance of the wordplay. It remains my favourite of the theme answers.
I just read the creator’s bio. 16?! My respect for Mason’s clever wordplay just increased.

Whoa, and wow!  Talk about wordplay!  Finding rhyming pairs for the clues that are synonyms to the “X or Y” pairs in the answers!

Not only that, but wicked originality.  The mind that came up with this elegant concept – and so excellently executed it – well, I’m very happy that it crossed paths with crosswords.  All this, mind you, from a 16-year-old who discovered our sweet pastime a year ago -- a year ago!  And no, this theme has never been done before in any of the major crossword outlets.  

My big hope is that you have caught the constructing bug, Mason, so that we can see what that fertile mind of yours percolates up the road.  Thank you for a splendid debut!

ITCH/INCH was interesting, and AISLOP seemed quite fresh.

Nice enough puzzle, but didn't feel like a Wednesday, either thematically or as clued. Finished very quickly (for me).

Congrats on the debut.

My first note has got to be "a 16-year-old is constructing a puzzle that appears in the NYTimes?  Wow!  I stand in awe."

But, having said that, I have to also be honest and say,  "Good puzzle?  For me, not so much."

The fill was relatively simple, so it wasn't a problem that I didn't understand the theme or the gimmick.  I wasn't concerned; I knew it would come in time.

The themed answers became relatively easy to infer once you had about half the letters.  I still didn't understand the theme.  Would it come to me eventually?

I finished the puzzle in below average Wednesday time, and I still didn't understand the theme.

I took a few minutes to re-examine the themed clues.  Oh, now I see?
Do I say, "That was really clever!"?
No, I said, "Yeah, I see it. OK, let's move on."

Underwhelmed by the puzzle.  Amazed by the constructor.
I hope that makes sense.
Congratulations, Mason Hyunjin Lee.  I'm sure you'll continue to improve.
Relaxing puzzle.Coasted.

I think I'll speak for many when I say I found this a "meh" puzzle for the solver, but a BRILLIANT showing by the constructor!.

I really wanted 35A to be ST THEODOSIOUS RUSSIAN ORTHODOX CATHEDRAL (Tremont, Cleveland, Ohio); in three letters, THAT would be a rebus!

This comment probably won't get by the emus but I quit as soon as I saw a clue that referenced Kobe Bryant.

I was very good friends with a deputy in the Vail police department so I was privy to a lot of inside information. 

I'll leave it at that.

Enjoyed the puzzle, but breezed through it and I’m not that good at crosswords yet (getting better). I liked the theme, and some of the wordplay made me smile. 

One nit: AISlop for me is not really “low-quality”, or it doesn’t quite capture the nefarious nature of it. It’s more over-produced, too busy, and has a certain quality that I hope we all are beginning to recognize. In fact, part of the problem is that it doesn’t look “low-quality” so people use it instead of an actual artist who knows what they’re doing and can actually lift the concepts the art is meant to convey.
Didn't understand the theme but this puzzle was still EASYPEASY

I had entered CAKE OR DEATH as the last theme entry, and it still made sense to me 😅
Long live Eddie Izzard!

Cute theme. 
Never heard of a SKOR before so that was the only look up.
Fun!
The surrounding fill could have been dialed up a bit but it's a clever theme clue/answer set and a very good debut.  Nicely done.

This one was quite simple but it was enjoyable. There was a lot of Gen-Z/Gen-Alpha terminology which I guess is where the challenge would come if you don't fit the demographic.

Regardless, a great debut imo. Congratulations!

What a really, really nifty theme!  And can you believe that this is a debut puzzle and that it's from a 16-year-old?  Amazing!!!

Normally I'm unaware of stuff like that.  But today's theme sent me scampering over to the Constructor's Notes on the Wordplay Blog.  Because I wanted to know HOW.  How does one come up with a theme like this?  Is it possible to think of an in-the-language "either/or"  phrase and then come up with two words to clue each half -- words that differ from each other by just the first letter?  That would be awesome.  

Or do you come up with two words that differ from each other by just the first letter -- and then find an in-the-language "either/or" phrase that fits the clues.  That would be awesome too.

Alas, Mason doesn't tell us.

As a constructor, I cannot imagine that I would be able to think even one of these theme answers up.  I'm pretty darned awed, Mason.  I'm glad to have stumbled upon your age;  I think you have a remarkable crossword-constructing career ahead of you.  As for solving this puzzle -- I pretty much needed the first two letters of each answer before I could fill it in, although I did first try to solve each one with no crosses.  Not an especially hard puzzle, but a very, very good one!

This was a Slumdog Millionaire puzzle for me. SKOR was my favorite candy bar, every August we headed to KMART for school clothes, Pong was our first gaming console, and four sports "gimmes" made this a fun, easy Wednesday. Congrats, creator, on a very nice debut puzzle!

Such a good puzzle! Very neat, tight clues. Absolutely love 
"Feathered accessory that won't constrict you"
Super easy-breezy, easy-peasy Wednesday puzzle, thank you!
Congratulations, Mason, and thank you for getting AISLOP into the NYTXW!  It’s such a terse and perfect term.

The comments here are very kind. I PR'd without being particularly focused. This makes me sad. It was too simple, and the range of clues, vocabulary, and knowledge one expects on a W were very lacking.

I am mainly here to vigorously decry the clue for 11A--"Helpful skill for guessers."  
ESP is not a skill.  Skills can be learned, developed, honed. 
ESP is an ability that people/scientists/etc. still struggle to understand.  It is usually described as a "purported ability" or a "pseudo-science" --and debunked.

I do not fully understand it myself, but I have experienced precognition (the earliest event when I was 6 years old), and with certain individuals I have experienced telepathy--despite not knowing the person, or not knowing them at all well.  Both can be upsetting.  ESP is certainly not an "on-demand" experience in the way a skill is available to one who has studied or developed it.  
(Attempts to "test" for ESP ignore the fact that it's not volitional.)

Good "guessers" are usually divergent thinkers with "quick wits"--able to process and assess rapidly.  It's definitely an asset for a solver.

Whew. And Wow. Good long Wednesday workout for me, but catching on to the trick was a big help and that's always a nice touch. And that is just one really clever theme. And... 16 years old? Wow. And of course my puzzle find. A Monday from October 11, 2021 by Ben Pall. Four theme answers in that one: TAKEITORLEAVEIT LOVEITORHATEIT MOVEITORLOSEIT MAKEITORBREAKIT And was surprised to see that 3 of those answers were making their one and only appearance in any puzzle. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/11/2021&h=57a ....
Fun puzzle, love rhe theme and the clue wordplay!  Congrats, Mason!
You ever get a puzzle correctly yet no know the theme? I couldn't figure this one out. Fun puzzle though.
Great job, Mason! Really fun Wednesday puzzle. Looking forward to your future submissions.
Really fun and creative! Nice start to Wednesday!

Breezy puzzle, that was solvable without having to figure out the theme.  

I like the concept of the CHEATMEAL. I’ve had a few without knowing there was a name for them.  

I was happy to see one of my favorite songs, Wicked Game by Chris ISAAK, make the cut.  I’m a sucker for a tenor.
Congrats on the debut! Nice lil puzzle you got there. More please!

I like the puzzle a lot but will add a criticism to preserve my fragile insecurities at somebody doing a clever thing half my age.

"Too easy.  It didnt take me five hours and now I have to do my real job instead of staring at my phone wondering what the clues mean"

That was fake criticism it was a solid fun puzzle.
Very fun cluing and entries in this puzzle! I enjoyed it a lot. Thanks, Mason.
Very fun puzzle! Finding "x or y" phrases that could also be clued in this specific way could not have been easy
Swift, breezy solve. A nice debut, and a terrific Monday puzzle!
Who needs a mnemonic to remember the cardinal points of a compass? And I like my waffles to be soggy with maple syrup.
A fun first entry. Nice work, Mason! I enjoyed solving this one.

Loved this puzzle! Perfect for a Wednesday, with a clever theme that actually helps you make the solve!

Rating:
10/10
This was fun! Thanks!
GREAT FIRST EFFORT! Hope you come back with another!
great puzzle! my first solve under 30 minutes: it felt so fluid. i do these to practice my english so i'm really proud! cheers :)
If I had an extra hand, I'd give this one three thumbs up.  Kudos to Mason.
Amazing work for your first puzzle—at 16 years old, no less! Perhaps it would have been better suited for a Monday or Tuesday, but a fun fill nonetheless.
This was fun! I'm pretty new to the crossword and don't know a lot of pop culture, but I was able to figure this out without the hunts from this page.

Emus ate my comment last night.  Breezy puzzle with a solid theme.  I had never heard of CHEATMEALs.  

I did like seeing one of my favorite songs, Chris ISAAK’s Wicked Game, crop up.  I’m a sucker for a tenor.
Really fun. Great job!

I think that this puzzle could’ve been for a Tuesday but overall this was fun once I figured out the theme. About half of the answers I erased and rewrote and some I took a complete guess. This is the first Wednesday I have solved without cheating. Very enjoyable.
is anyone actually practicing Taebo these days??

When I got black mamba, I thought to myself this is going to be a fun puzzle. I don’t know how you’re 18 years old and I’m 51 but this was written just for me my best Wednesday ever.

After I did a breeder research, I’d love to get to know more about making these puzzles because this one looks great and I would like you to know that this is the puzzle that started me on a path

Thank you Mason Lee
IMO the cluing for 54D is a little sus, to use modern parlance.
Fun puzzle!

Brit here. Enjoyed this one and raised an eyebrow when I saw my home city mentioned.

Never heard that K word before but I won't forget it now 😂
Good one!
A sixteen-year-old who only discovered crosswords a year ago already has one accepted by the NYT?? Bright future ahead for this kid.
referring to a Adam as the start of the caucasian race is... no fun another other clue for Adam would be okay 'first in a race' that's forcing a belief down others brains.  fun puzzle nonetheless

My fastest Wednesday in the last two years (since I've been on XWSTATS). Might be the fastest of all time -- I have a faster solve on NYT.com but it was not a real Wednesday puzzle; it was a special puzzle that somehow got counted as a Wednesday result. I've always wondered if I could ever beat that time on a legit Wednesday. Would have been close if I knew how to spell Mickey ROaRKE's name!

Big booboo I think...
Without further......adieu....not ado.(even though it might sound the same) Adieu i..Unless that's being accepted these days...
My fastest Wednesday is now just 6 seconds slower than my fastest Tuesday.

Today I completed the Crossword in 16:15! Can you solve it too?

Try it out!

wsj://games/crosswords?mode=WSJ_puzzles_share

truly special WSJ puzzle I'm a fan of crosswords this one is a beauty don't miss it - truly brilliant sets the bar check it out!
AISLOP debut goes hard, lol
New Wednesday best at 5:30. Also 11 seconds faster than my best Tuesday.
Way to go, Mason! This was so much fun. AI Slop had me crying laughing at my desk.

Congratulations, Mason, on a very fine debut!!🙌🏼🥳

I'm in awe that you are only 16 years old and already have accomplished what has eluded so many promising constructors who have long been veterans of solving these puzzles!! I loved your theme -- SO clever and I would not have been able to tease it out easily if I had not first read Sam's Wordplay column.

Again, well done -- I look forward to seeing more from you!😊

P.S. I have a feeling those SATs will be or were no sweat for you.😉

I didn’t even realize puzzles could be submitted until I saw the comments here. And I only came to the comments to say that any puzzle with ALSLOP as an answer is ok with me!

Really enjoyed the puzzle. Congrats Mason, and thank you for sharing it!
I loved those themed clues, Mason!! If you have more of them that you weren't able to use in the puzzle, please share :)
Nice puzzle, but felt more like a Tuesday in difficulty. Lots of record setting, myself included, judging from the comments.
Spoiler: I have obviously been on the internet faaaar too long as my first thought was CAKEORDEATH rather than the eventual correct answer.
Great submission from a 16-year-old! But it was more suited to a Monday in terms of difficulty level.

Mason, when I was 16 years old 66 years ago I was just beginning to struggle with crosswords often while waiting for a loop at the Seawane Golf Cub in Hewlett Harbor, NY.  The Daily News, the Mirror and the Long Island Press but never the NYT. I started the NYT puzzles in 1966 in college after I got back from Vietnam. Thousands of puzzles later, yours is certainly one I will never forget. Thank you!

fun and very satisfying, authentic. No AI SLOP here! 
great stuff.
@Ethan here everyone is a winner 😀

@Josh
BRAIN fog is bad enough.

@Josh
All we needed was RFK Jr and we'd have the complete set!
@Meghan. And don’t forget that good old standby “Chi.”

@Meghan

And X-ray.
And X-rated.
;-)

@Meghan
If you can't sign your name, make your mark: X.

@sotto voce
But those are just the letter X. Not the same thing, seems to me.
You might want to read the Wordplay column...

@Douglas Laycock

I would really like to seek to understand and help, I am not trying to be belittling or snarky.
1) Are you talking about the theme, or some other clues? If other clues, which? The only one that seemed off to me today was AURA because I don’t use it that way, but apparently some do.
2) If the theme then:
a) did you not read the column and if not why not read it before commenting?
b) did you read the column but still not understand?
c) in either case, did you want to complain that if a theme needs a column to explain it then it’s not a good theme? (Because that’s potentially a legitimate complaint; although in this case I think it’s pretty straightforward—which is not a diss on you because we’ve all slapped our head sometimes and not figured out a theme that seems painfully obvious to everyone else, it happens)
@SP AURA as clued today is either Gen Z or Gen Alpha slang. I’m familiar with it from my teenage daughter.
@Douglas Laycock At least you got three people to respond condescendingly.

@Steve L I know you want to paint this as a "me" thing, but I have seen multiple people call out your condescending comments.

When people want to help, they say things like:

"Check the wordplay column. It should be in there."
"What are you having trouble with?"

When people want to be condescending, they say things like:

"Did you not read the column?"
"Would you like to tell us which clues you need help with, or are you going to leave us hanging?"

It's not "three people are just trying to help and one person is just trying to start trouble." There's a pattern here, and it happens a lot.

@SP It's definitely common for online bullies to state that the people pointing out their bullying behavior are "just trying to stir up trouble" or "a bot." All you guys need is to say I'm texting from my mother's basement, and you'll hit the trifecta!

But obviously you have the same right to say that I am just trying to stir up trouble as I do to say that you're condescending and doing a lot of gatekeeping.
@Jane Wheelaghan Understandable. I have seen other people call them out. That's why I pushed back on their assertion/deflection that it was something only I had a problem with. YMMV.

@Jane Wheelaghan and Matt

Thanks, Jane, for your support. And Matt, I will say that indeed there are a couple of people like you—one of whom goes by a singe letter and lives in the Midwest—who genuinely don’t get how the regulars interact here. That doesn’t make that small minority right. All it means is that your minds are made up to be contrarian.

@Jane Wheelaghan

Thanks, Jane.

I swear to God I don't know what this guy expects.

I asked if I could help and I made a little joke.  Was it snarky?  Maybe, but quite often *my* posts are "cries from the heart".  I just have to write something to get the frustration off my chest.

So I was asking if he really wanted help.  Barry also gave him a suggestion that was short and to the point.  If he had asked a specific question Barry would have helped him.  But he didn't, so Barry did the next best thing and directed him to Wordplay.

SP then subtly admonished Barry and I (it's quite all right, SP, you are a better forum citizen that I am, and I acknowledge that), and yet we *still* didn't get any further, specific, questions.

I honestly don't know what to do anymore.

@Steve L I think the people who have called you out absolutely understand how the regulars interact here.

I've mentioned to you before that you appear to place a high value on being "right." It was very interesting that you felt the need to point out that I'm not "right" just  because there's another poster who also annoys you. (Now you've acknowledged that two people have called you out for being condescending. It's progress. If only you could acknowledge the others).

You're not all sharing the same brain, but wow. The comment from Francis -- "I tried so hard to help him and he won't reply to me! What do I do?" -- This happens all the time! 

What do you think is actually more likely? That you and Barry and SP and Francis all snarkily saying the same thing was a great idea, or that all these people you guys do this to see all the condescending comments and decide not to bother?

I've seen it several times on this board. SP gave me like 40 minutes to respond, then posted a stand-alone comment calling me out by name. I asked the other Matt to leave me alone, and he commented on my post about how he couldn't respond to me. Now Francis waits a few hours and posts a screed wondering why someone won't respond to him. It's seriously so weird. Why do you care so much about what people who don't even know you say to you on here? I'm flabbergasted every day, but I can't look away.

@Francis

Ignore him. That is all.

@Jeff B MD

For a split second, I had MARY. You’ve heard the story…
Lol

@john ezra

Yeah, that was a pretty hunky Vietnamese wedding, eh?

Had I read the column first before commenting I would not have been so utterly boorish in my comments about it. Reviewing the puzzle, I'm willing to revise my initial thoughts and do think the cluing and some of the fill is really quite good, and I'm not pulling an Infantino here, but any penalties given to this puzzle are hereby suspended for at least a year and its eligibility has been restored.

@Andrzej

I thought is was an acronym for Expert at Solving Puzzles, so I had no issue.

@john ezra
I remember thinking 'those guys would be drinking Iron City, not Rolling Rock.'

@Andrzej, no, I'm backing down. As a parent whose kids were once 16, I know that encouragement and praise have a great impact on a teenager's psyche and the same is true for criticism and negativity. You and I can shrug off the negatives because our characters are fully formed and realized, but that's not true for Mason, no matter how smart or emotionally stable he is, so I do regret not realizing his age before making comments. But that's just me, I'm not telling anyone else what to do.

@Barry Ancona
I agree on all counts. This would have been a nice Tuesday puzzle, but it is a very impressive debut for any day of the week!

@Weak

@Petrol

I knew this, and I knew that I knew this.
What I don't know is whether it's true in other languages.
Anyone who knows want to clarify?

@The X-Phile
When I was learning Spanish, I instinctively used the order correctly in sentences such a the ones described. In that language, and probably all Romance languages, the same sort of rules apply.

@Petrol

Blind Three Mice.  Bad big wolf.
@Petrol For a deeper dive: https://www.perfect-english-grammar.com/order-of-adjectives.html

@The X-Phile
I definitely remember studying chapters on this very complicated topic in my French classes.

Evidemment, in French, the adjective usually follows the noun, which makes the whole thing even more complicated. Of course, there are also exceptions to that rule. 

The Tour do France will award "the maillot jaune" to the winner--as discussed recently for another puzzle. "Jaune" is yellow, of course.

@Don H

And as a counterpoint to what I just replied to Times Rita above, if there weren't a discrepancy in some languages, there would be no need for a web page like that one.

@Vaer

Go granny go granny go granny go!

@Petrol

Thank you for this post. Stuff you never think about as a native speaker. 

One of the hardest parts of learning a new language is asking a native speaker how to say something and why it follows a particular grammar rule and their answer is “it just sounds right“ or “the other way sounds wrong” or “that is grammatically correct, but people just don’t talk like that”

Mad ups to anyone that can speak multiple languages, that aren’t spoken in their home growing up.  Maybe I should say. Learn a new language after say the old ripe, er ripe old, age of 18.

@The X-Phile A related issue is Time & Place. For example, in English, "I'll be in NYC at 4:00pm." (place before time, always). In German, "I'll be at 4:00pm in NYC." (time before place, always). Not sure how it is in other languages.

@Steve L
On the other hand, there are those cases in Spanish where the adjective does get placed differently, leading to a slightly different interpretation.
@Steve L Uh-oh. I sense an oxford comma debate. A comma after "fish" would have cleared up any ambiguity. The clue can be read either way; in fact I think PuzzleDog's interpretation is correct.

@Michael G

My version has [Fish, roads or weapons that are long and thin].

Is yours different?

An Oxford comma would go after “roads”; “fish” already has one. 

And I still can’t see how etymology fits in at all.

@Steve L. My bad. I meant a comma after "roads." 

"Fish, roads, or weapons that are long and thin" would make it clear that "long and thin" referred only to "weapons."

@Michael G

But that’s not what is meant. All of them are long and thin, and that’s why they’re grouped together. (Plus, of course, that they’re all PIKES).

@Petrol You’re not a pedant, just wrong. “At it” is used frequently in this context.

@Jane Wheelaghan

You might know of KMART from the movie Rain Man, in which the titular character, played by Dustin Hoffman, repeatedly asserts that “KMART (does what a vacuum cleaner does).”

And it did. It’s pretty much out of business now, although there might be a couple still hanging on somewhere. 

In the Outer Banks of North Carolina, where we have a beach house, there were for the longest time just two major retailers: Walmart and KMART. KMART was closer, but Walmart was better. When we needed something for the house, the wife would always say she was going to the Marts brothers. 

The KMART is now a Target.

@Steve L

The American middle class had subsets usually defined by median household income, but an easier way was to just divide the group into thirds by where they shopped: 

KMart
Walmart
Target

The phrase is "without further ado". Think of a host chitchatting with the audience, talking up the program or the speaker that's coming. "Without further ado, I present to you ". They're done making an ado and they're getting to the main event.

Angela, They may sound the same (to some people), but they have different meanings. The Midi phrase calls for ADO. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/ado https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/adieu
